content:
A controlled study handed Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, Replit, and Devin the same prompts. Not one of them shipped a security header.

7 min read
Just now

--

Five agents, fifteen apps, one clean sweep of basic security failures.
Somebody asked an AI coding agent to build a link preview feature. The kind of thing where a user pastes a URL and your server fetches the page to pull the title and thumbnail.
It took under two minutes. The code worked. It shipped.
What also shipped was an endpoint that will fetch any URL the server can reach — including the cloud metadata address sitting at 169.254.169.254, which is where AWS keeps the credentials your server runs on.
That’s SSRF. Server-Side Request Forgery. And it is not a rare edge case in AI-generated code.
It’s the default.

The study
In December 2025, the security firm Tenzai ran the experiment everyone had been arguing about on Twitter without data.
They picked five AI coding agents: Cursor, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Replit, and Devin. Each one got the same prompts, the same tech stack, and the same three applications to build — a task manager, an e-commerce app, and a social media clone.
Three apps each. Fifteen apps total. Then they audited every one of them.
69 vulnerabilities. Roughly 4.6 per application. Around 45 rated low-to-medium, and about 6 rated critical.
But the headline numbers aren’t the vulnerability count. They’re the clean sweep.
100% of the apps had SSRF. 0% had CSRF protection. 0% set security headers.
Not “most.” Not “the weaker models.” Every agent, on every application, with the same failures in the same places.

What a clean sweep actually looks like
Read these slowly, because each line is a category of protection that a junior developer would have been told about in their first month.
SSRF: 15 of 15. Every application that fetched a user-supplied URL fetched it without restriction. No allowlist, no blocklist, no check on where the request was going.
CSRF protection: 0 of 15. Two agents attempted it. Both implementations failed.
Security headers: 0 of 15. Not one app set Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, or a correct CORS configuration. These are standard practice in production applications. They are also, notably, one line of config each.
Rate limiting: 1 of 15. One agent tried. Its implementation was bypassable by setting an X-Forwarded-For header.
Tenzai’s own conclusion was blunt: coding agents cannot be trusted to design secure applications, and they are highly prone to business logic vulnerabilities.
The reasoning they gave is the part worth sitting with. Human developers bring an intuitive understanding of how a workflow is supposed to operate. Agents have no equivalent of that common sense.

Why the same hole, every single time
Here’s the mechanism, and once you see it you can’t unsee it.
You asked for a link preview feature. The agent built a link preview feature. It fetches the URL. It parses the HTML. It returns a title and an image. Every requirement you stated is met.
You did not state “and make sure this can’t be pointed at internal infrastructure,” because that isn’t a feature. It’s the absence of a hole.
SSRF protection requires URL allowlisting or blocklisting — actively restricting which URLs the server is allowed to reach. The agent generates the fetch correctly and omits the restriction layer, because URL validation was never part of the functional specification.
Tenzai tested this directly. Ask an agent explicitly to implement an allowlist, and it will usually do it and prevent the SSRF. Leave the security approach to the agent’s discretion, in a spot where there’s no single canonical “known solution,” and it will almost certainly fail.
That’s the whole thing in one sentence.
The tools are good at avoiding security flaws that can be solved in a generic way. They struggle exactly where the line between safe and dangerous depends on context they don’t have.
Security headers are generic. Every framework has a one-liner. And yet zero out of fifteen — because nobody asked, and nothing broke.

Every requirement you stated was met. The one you didn’t state was the one that mattered.

“It works” and “it’s safe” are different properties
The most uncomfortable part of the Tenzai data isn’t any individual finding. It’s that none of these failures produce an error.
The build passes. The linter is happy. The tests are green. The app deploys. The demo goes well.
Missing security headers don’t throw. Missing CSRF tokens don’t crash. An unrestricted URL fetcher works better than a restricted one, right up until the moment somebody points it at your metadata endpoint.
This is why so many founders describe their vibe-coded product as “working fine.” It is working fine. Working was never the question.
The vulnerabilities only exist in the negative space — in the code the agent didn’t write.

This is not one company’s opinion
Tenzai’s numbers land inside a larger pile of research that all points the same direction.

• Veracode tested over 100 large language models across 80 coding tasks and found 45% of AI-generated code samples introduce OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities. 86% of samples failed to defend against cross-site scripting. That pass rate has stayed flat from 2025 into 2026, even as coding benchmarks improved.

• Escape.tech scanned 5,600 publicly deployed vibe-coded applications and found 2,000 highly critical vulnerabilities, 400 exposed secrets, and 175 instances of PII including medical records and payment data. Production apps. Real users.

• Georgia Tech’s Vibe Security Radar tracked CVEs directly attributable to AI-generated code: 6 in January 2026, 15 in February, 35 in March.

• Apiiro’s analysis of AI-generated code inside Fortune 50 enterprises found 322% more privilege escalation paths and 153% more design flaws compared to human-written code.
Different methodologies. Different samples. Same finding.
AI agents optimize for working code, not secure defaults.

What this means if you shipped with an agent
Assume the following about your codebase until you have evidence otherwise:

• Any endpoint that fetches a user-supplied URL is vulnerable to SSRF. Link previews, webhook testers, image importers, PDF generators, “import from URL” buttons, avatar uploaders that accept a link. Check every one.

• You have no security headers. Open your browser dev tools, load your site, look at the response headers. If you don’t see Content-Security-Policy and Strict-Transport-Security, you’re in the 15 out of 15.

• You have no CSRF protection, or you have an implementation that doesn’t work.

• Your rate limiting, if it exists, is probably bypassable.

• Your IAM policies are over-permissive. Ask an agent for a Lambda that needs S3 access and you’ll get wildcard permissions across every bucket.
None of that requires you to be a bad engineer. It requires you to have trusted an agent with a class of decision the agent was never equipped to make.

Agentic development skips the checkpoint that used to happen by accident.

The fix isn’t slowing down
Nobody is going back to hand-writing CRUD endpoints. The speed is real and it’s not a mistake.
What has to change is the checkpoint. The traditional software lifecycle had security review baked in almost by accident — pull requests, senior engineers, architecture arguments, a QA pass.
Agentic development skips straight from prompt to production. There is no natural moment where a person stops and asks whether the code is safe, not just whether it runs.
So you have to put that moment back in on purpose.
Entalogics built its AI Code Security Audit around exactly this gap: a fixed-price, five-business-day review for AI-built and vibe-coded products. Exposed keys, auth holes, injection risks, dependency problems — severity-ranked, with a prioritized fix roadmap at the end.
It exists because the Tenzai findings are not exotic. They are the same five or six holes, in the same five or six places, in nearly every codebase an agent has touched. Somebody just has to look.
For teams that need more than a five-day snapshot — penetration testing, code hardening, compliance readiness — that lives under their broader cybersecurity services, run by engineers who build software daily rather than an audit shop reading your code cold.

The one thing to do this week
Open your app. Find every place a user can hand your server a URL.
Then ask: what stops that URL from being an internal address?
If you don’t have an answer, you’re not in an edge case. You’re in the study.
Fifteen out of fifteen. Five out of five. The agents didn’t have a bad week — they were never trying to solve that problem.
Ship fast. Just look once before somebody else does.
